replies.content:
(Tolkienguy)

If I remember right (you probably know more about this than me, JMG), Geomancy was developed during the Islamic era, so you'd want modern Persian, also called new Persian.

The eras of Persian roughly break down as follows:
Old Persian-the Persian of the Achaemenid dynasty. This era ends with Alexander the Great's conquests of the Persian empire, after which Greek replaced Persian as the area's main literary language for a time. Old Persian compares to modern Persian about the same way Latin compares to French, ie its a totally different and much more grammatically complicated language.

Middle Persian-The literary language of the Parthian and Sassanid empires (along with Aramaic). The Manichean scriptures were originally written in Middle Persian.

After the Arab conquest of the Sassanid Empire in the late 7th century, Middle Persian gradually dies out as a written language, and pretty much every Persian writer from about AD 750 to AD 900 or so uses Arabic. After AD 900, the Arab Caliphate begins to weaken and the former Persian empire comes under the rule of a series of local dynasties founded by native Persian nobles, who revive Persian as a court language. This form of Persian is called New or Modern Persian; it was written in Arabic script, had extensive Arabic loanwords, and had lost the Latin-style grammatical inflections Old Persian had. As one might guess from the name, its is essentially the same language spoken by educated people in Iran and Afghanistan today-standard written Persian has changed very little since AD 1100 or so, though it has a lot of colloquial dialects which are different.

replies.title: Re: “Conjunctio”: Geomantic Dialectic
replies.content:
Early modern Persianate geomancy happens to be one of my specialities as a historian of science, though unfortunately there's no one else currently working on it, as far as I know, for the usual colonialist-orientalist reasons. But there is indeed a large corpus of Persian geomantic manuals -- the canon standard in 16th-century Mughal India, for example, includes over 50 -- of which none have been edited or studied by historians, though several remain in circulation among practitioners in Iran today, and new works on the subject continue to be written, especially by ayatollahs (including one of the teachers of Khomeini himself). Note that both Persian and Latin geomantic texts derive from Arabic ones; the earliest extant such texts were composed by folks like al-Zanati and, yes, Ibn Mahfuf in the early 13th century. Given heavy imperial patronage, however, Persian geomantic texts came to be the most sophisticated in the Western tradition. Latin ones are somewhat rudimentary by comparison, since the translators often garbled or simply dropped bits they didn't understand, and the two traditions increasingly diverged from there.

As such, one of my middle-term plans is to put out translations of some of the most popular Persian manuals as soon as my work schedule permits -- JMG's convinced me that folks might actually buy them! In the meantime, I have a preliminary survey of the Persian geomantic tradition, which identifies some of the most important authors and texts that will need to be investigated, and which can be downloaded here:

https://www.academia.edu/19579995/Persianate_Geomancy_from_%E1%B9%AC%C5%ABs%C4%AB_to_the_Millennium_A_Preliminary_Survey

As for learning enough Persian for such texts to begin to make sense, I'm afraid you'd have to get a thorough grounding in classical Arabic first (takes about ten years), as well as some background in the history of Islamic philosophy and science, and Islamic studies more generally. After all, that's one of the reasons orientalists have almost totally ignored geomancy and most of the other occult sciences until recently: not only do they run afoul of our materialist orthodoxy, but they're also really hard! ;)

replies.title: Re: “Conjunctio”: Geomantic Dialectic
replies.content:
Sure thing -- it's amazingly useful stuff, with all sorts of operations never developed in the Latinate tradition, and needs to get out more! As mental salvation from drudgery, I recommend just starting with Arabic and seeing how it tastes, since much of what you'd want in Persian in terms of occult science was in Arabic first (though often more sophisticated yet accessible in later Persian formulation). To prepare for eventually getting into hard-core Islamic occult theory, start with Wheeler Thackston's An Introduction to Koranic and Classical Arabic and take things from there. Then if you still want to do Persian, Thackston's Introduction to Persian is likewise the place to start.

As for geomantic texts in various Islamicate vernaculars like Urdu, most definitely, there's plenty. As far as I can tell though, they hew pretty closely to the Persian model of circa 1600. And no, I haven't come across any translations into Sanskrit -- in the great Mughal translation movement the direction was usually Sanskrit to Persian, not vice versa, with Urdu coming out of that culture of synthesis.

There is one of the great cultures (in the Spenglerian sense) that went above and beyond in its use of the collective emotions of the crowd of spectators, to such an extent that I think it's fair to say that its image of society (analogous to the Magian community of believers, the Apollonian polis of citizens, or the Faustian lone prophet preaching to the masses) *was* the city collected as the crowd at the sports game and/or public spectacle; said great culture also went so far as to enshrine its premier sports game in a myth that survived a thousand years.

That great culture is, of course, Mesoamerica, with the deities and egregores that go with it.

replies.title: Re: Ibuprofen
replies.content:
boulderchum, I happen to be a physician. If I may, I would like to warn you away from routine (esp. daily) use of ibuprofen, or any NSAID (Non-Steroidal AntiInflammatory Drug). Depending on your age, esp. over 45, it elevates your risk of high blood pressure, stroke, heart attack and irreversible kidney disease. For any age, it elevates your risk of gastro-intestinal bleeding. When I was a hospitalist, these drugs were absolutely verboten for in-patients with any risk factors. I once prescribed a short course to a healthy (notwithstanding her injury) outpatient of mine, a woman in her early 40's. At the end of her course, she developed a massive stomach bleed, was rushed to the hospital and nearly died. Her bleed was attributed to the ibuprofen I prescribed.

Ibuprofen, and similar (esp. naproxen, usually sold under the trade name Aleve) drugs are sold over the counter (OTC). That does not mean they are safe! I think they are far too risky to take for everyday pains like headache, muscle ache and the like.

Sounds like you’re saying, “I’ve just practised for the greater good, to benefit all sentient beings, now I’d like something back for my efforts.”

Spiritual practise, and especially Buddhism, is to release egoic desires which are the cause of suffering.

In order to receive, you need to give unconditionally, from your heart, without any expectation of receiving anything in return.

I’m not sure if you align with a specific Boddhisatva, but perhaps Green Tara is a deity you may consider working with?

When you see, feel and understand yourself as being whole, there will be nothing that you need to add to yourself, nor will there be nothing that can take away from your True Nature.

OM TARE TU TARE TURE SOHA

~ Tanya

comments.title: Past Lives
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
A couple of weeks ago you very kindly responded to my question about obsessive privacy and secrecy in daily life, suggesting that I consider possible past life experiences as a spy or a resistance worker. Given that other people reported the same issue, I am taking the liberty of recounting how I got on with that consideration, even though it’s not strictly to do with magic.
It’s generally accepted that fears and traumas of all kinds leave traces in the body and the nervous system. A number of therapies exist to deal with such problems, many energy-based. One which I have used is usually called the Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT) or more popularly « tapping. » There are many variations but all make use of points on the body corresponding to organ meridians in Chinese medicine. You generally begin by finding a setup phrase which describes what you understand as your condition (eg « I’m angry about the way my parents treated me that time ») and see what physical sensations it produces and where. Then, tapping in sequence through the points, you start with a permissive formula : « Even though I’m angry …. » etc, finishing with a positive statement like « I deeply and completely accept myself. » (Quite why this works is unclear, but it does). You then go round the points several times, and usually the emotions and the physical discomfort are reduced, or even eliminated.
More recently, Steve Wells, an Australian EFT practitioner has developed other verbal formulas to use while tapping, and even when tapping is not practically possible. For example: « I release all my emotional attachments to » (fear, trauma etc.). This reflects the fact that we often can’t let go of such things voluntarily or automatically.
Now then, it struck me that if these techniques are effective against trauma in our current embodied existence, they should ideally be equally useful against inherited past-life traumas, not least as a diagnostic tool.
The first step was to simply relax, close my eyes, and repeat something like « I release all my emotional attachments to memories of having been a spy in a past life. » (The effect of repeated statements of this kind equates quite quickly to being in a meditative state). I got an immediate strong, physical reaction. Without boring you with the detail, I was able to the narrow this down to a period (WW2), and to a year (1943), by working systematically through the dates, and checking several times that I got the same reaction from the same inputs. Likewise for places: Paris (quite strong) Berlin (stronger) Brussels (very strong and very negative: it’s a city I’ve always disliked). Out of interest, I tried Buchenwald, a concentration camp where many non-German resistance workers were sent. That got a strong reaction, but apparently I didn’t die there - that was in Paris in 1948.
Obviously all this is highly subjective, but the reactions are consistent (becoming less pronounced over time, as you’d expect, because of the healing effect of repetition) and the story that emerges is coherent, and consistent with known historical facts and personal experiences (I avoided learning German when I should have, for example, though I speak fluent French). I’ve been treating these « recovered » traumas with EFT and the results seem promising. In the last few days I’ve been trying to find when and where I was born in that incarnation, and I think I have pinned it down to a place and a year. It’s time-consuming, but it does seem to work.

replies.title: Re: Leland
replies.content:
Leland was definitely a life-long occultist. He's also the one who published the classic "Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches of Italy" about the same time as he published "The Mystic Will" (original title: "Have You a Strong Will?"). He doesn't do the high-level abstract theorizing of Levi or Blavatsky, but he's deep and profound without being fancy, and he was very widely read in Renaissance magic as well as a life-long student of folk magic from its folk practitioners. IMHO, he's enormously important, and almost completely ignored.

Incidentally, his "The Alternate Sex, or the Female Intellect in Man and the Masculine in Woman" anticipates Jung's notion of the anima/animus. Jung knew about Leland, and I have always wondered whether he may have happened on that book in his early days.

replies.title: Re: Leland
replies.content: Yep. Robert M.'s comment above is about what I would have written; Leland was up to his eyeballs in occultism, and he'll feature in my series of posts about American magic.

2) I couldn't possibly speak to the entire body of Irish archetypes, but the most prevalent one I can think of that would correspond most smoothly to the American Changer, the German Wild Hunt, the more broadly European Ragnarök, or the British Warring Dragons (what CSL points to in That Hideous Strength as the cycle between Britain and Logres, or Arthur and Mordred)--the archetype that orchestrates periods of national change in Ireland--would be that of the Invasion as depicted in the Lebor Gabála Érenn and the Cath Maige Tuired. Successive waves of aggressive newcomers that either take over the land entirely by contracting propitiously with Herself, or else that must be resisted at all costs, and nonetheless wreak irrevocable change across the island. Ireland's relationship with its neighbor island immediately to the east seems to reflect this archetype to a very great extent, at least over the past fifteen hundred years or so (from the fractious relationship hinted at by the Second Branch of Y Mabinogi on the British side of the Irish Sea, to the coming of Christianity--in a remarkably different capacity to that of the rest of Europe--to bloody Oliver Cromwell, to the Diaspora*, to the Easter Rising and the struggles of the past century).

*Incidentally, Dot, I don't know if you're inclined at all towards film but Martin Scorsese's 'Gangs of New York' illustrates brilliantly a playing out of the Irish archetypes of invasion when it's forced out of its homeland and repeats itself on American soil. The film essentially retells the two myths I cited above, using nineteenth century New York history and gang warfare as a backdrop, and casts Leonardo Dicaprio in the role of Lugh, Liam Neeson as Nuada, and Daniel Day-Lewis as Balor of the Evil Eye.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's difficult for me to offer specific advice without having any detailed examples to go on, but in my repeated experience, when people say this -- and I've heard it a good many times from various people -- what's actually going on is that the people involved have wildly unrealistic ideas about what actually constitutes "genuine, well-intentioned, extremely promising financial opportunities." There's an enormous amount of wishful thinking and economic misunderstanding out there, and it leads a lot of people to pour money down ratholes under the mistaken assumption that they're investing in something that's guaranteed to bring in a good return. One thing you might consider doing is making a list of these apparently promising opportunities, and seeing what they have in common. Another is that you might visit your local public library once it opens again, read a bunch of books about money management, and see if you can spot any misunderstandings about money that have become part of your family culture.

I'll pass on something related to invisibility spells. It comes from a witchcraft tradition, but you don't have to be a witch to use it.

When in sight of them, imagine that the space inside your head between your ears is filled with mashed potatoes. Soft, bland, featureless mashed potatoes. Keep it up until you are of sight.

replies.title: Re: Karmic accounting

It over looks a small square patch of patchy grass with a single crab-apple tree in the middle - I love looking at this tree. Within this apartment complex there are other larger areas with grass (in a similarly patchy state) with various shade trees. As far as I can tell the trees and grass haven't been watered for several weeks, which is troublesome because this is in the arid southwest & the weather has been typically dry (with the exception of one good rain shower about three weeks back) and slightly hotter than normal. The apartment complex was sold about three years ago and the new owners seem to be neglecting the property (inside & out) more & more. I don't know if repairs need to be done (but have been unavoidably delayed) to the sprinkler system or the groundskeeper were ordered to just stopped watering. I'm worried about the trees which are blessing of nature in the midst of a sea of concrete. I don't know if the management wants the trees to die or what. I do pour out some water for the crab-apple tree, but can't overdo it because management might notice and give me heck. I'm guessing that doing a divination to see if it would be a beneficial to contact the management* about the lack of watering. (I-Ching for me; quite a challenge but the only thing with which I have some familiarity.) Besides  prayer, is there anything that can be done to help save the crab-apple tree and other trees in the complex? I am open to suggestions that include simple natural/folk magic suitable for someone with no training. BTW, the long term 'plan' is to move to a different state to be closer to family (a lot of obstacles, mostly internal, need to be overcome to make this a reality & is a question for another MM).

replies.title: Re: Tree - endangered?
replies.content: Have you considered taking a pail of water out there and watering the tree yourself? That strikes me as the logical first step.

comments.title: Astrology (IV)
comments.content:
Good evening.
Following on from previous observations about events preceding their prediction in charts, of which we have seen some fine examples recently, I was thinking that perhaps the effect of a planet moving from one house to the next is less like a light switch, which is implied in most writings, and more like an electronic square wave, with a 2 or 3 degree slope. That is, the effect of a house remains constant (say a value of 100%) over the course of a planet's transit, except at the very end, when the effect of the next house begins and then rises over 2 to 3 degrees, while the effect of the current house drops away. So the two lines, added together equal 100%, but as one rises the other falls.
That would, I think, account for the fact that we have recently seen events unfold which take off a few days before the astrological event which charts them.
For one thing, your Aries Ingress (USA) predicted a strong populist movement, the 5 June Eclipse predicted turmoil, the Cancer Ingress predicted more turmoil, right before another eclipse, and here we are with a populist movement (not the one imagined), sweeping the globe in fact, with triggering events occurring just before the predictive event. So the the death of George Floyd happened just a few days before the eclipse and have triggered a massive populist rising, at least as big as the populist movement that got Mr Trump into the White House.

Bruce
AKA Renaissance Man

replies.content: That seems like a reasonable hypothesis. Let's see how it works over the next year or so, and draw some conclusions.

comments.title: Synchronicity
comments.content:
Funny you should have a quote on synchronicity.
For decades now, I've observed a curious phenomenon when out and about. I'll try and explain this in less than 10,000 words, but here goes.
As I walk down the street, when I see someone walking in front of me, walking slower than I am, there appears, more than half the time, a third person coming towards us and, again, almost invariable, we all seem to meet in the same 4m circle at the same time.
I have also noticed that, as I walk or cycle or drive down the block towards an intersection, again, slightly more than half the time, the intersection remains deserted the entire time I approach until the moment I reach it, and suddenly there is at least one other person or vehicle arriving from the cross-street. This is not so true of major roads, as there is always traffic coming and going, they have another feature, which I shall describe.
As I drive along, at the speed limit, I see a vehicle doing slightly less than the speed limit ahead, which means I approach them from behind. If traffic is open, that is, when there are gaps in the traffic, as I approach close enough to warrant changing lanes to pass, I observe behind me cars going faster than me which catch up to me at that exact moment and keep me from going around the slower driver. This happens about half the time.
These things happen day or night, summer or winter, no matter the weather, or any other conditions.
I've been meditating on this curious observation for decades now and I've got nothing. Just a sense that, somehow, something is going on, the universe is just not quite random enough.
And no, it isn't confirmation bias, these really are statistically improbable occurrences, I documented this for about two years just to be sure.

I know it isn't really a question, I just find a need to put that out there for contemplation and so someone else would be aware.

Bruce
AKA
Renaissance Man

replies.title: Re: Synchronicity
replies.content: That's really quite odd. Still, you're quite correct: the universe is not random. We're just not smart enough to be able to understand its order and pattern.

comments.title: Stagnation and Organization
comments.content:
Archdruid,

So over the past few weeks politics weighed heavily on my mind, and I've had several conversations with my friends about policing in the US, the military, and etc...

One particular theme I kept circling back toward, was that organizations of any kind seem to develop stagnation. Like a branch upon a tree that eventually ends instead of becoming a fully developed trunk. For example, I was explaining to a friend how I would like to see more military grade training for our police when dealing with deescalation and such, since the current programs seem involve power point presentations rather than actual "live fire" training. He pointed out that people are people everywhere and even the military largely has certification courses based on lectures, infact very little non-combat material is trained with "live fire" training.

This surprised me, but upon reflection it makes a great deal of sense. All things naturally seem to go for the path of least resistance unless there is a greater impulse pushing them to the path of greater resistance. Organizations don't invest in things that aren't considered part of the main flows, or trunk, so human laziness takes over.

Then I started to wonder how do magical lodges deal with this? It seems to me that every member in a group ritual would have to put in a great deal of time and energy to move the ritual forward, but the propensity to human laziness remains. How do y'all deal with that?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: Stagnation and Organization
replies.content: Magical lodges go through exactly the same cycle. What usually happens is that a lodge does amazing things, settles into stagnation (usually when the original founder dies), and then either goes under or gets new leadership that jolts it out of its stupor and gets it going again. Individual leaders can make a huge amount of difference.

One thing led to another and I find your blog and purchase some of your books. I was fascinating and devoured everything. Finally, I decided to join the AODA, which I did two years ago. I took off like a bullet, struggling a bit to practice consistently (being from such an intellectual and theological tradition, which highly emphasizes reading), but made some good headway, particularly with the sphere of protection.

In everything I have read and from what you’ve said, one of the most common testimonies is that beginning ritual practice initiates an intense process where unhandled problems rise to the surface, demanding to be dealt with, without which you cannot progress.

Following the self-initiation, I had a very strong sense already that something had just been set in motion that would require great changes. Perhaps six months into my practice, I began having increasing awareness that my “casual” drinking had gone well beyond that and had been that way for years without any true recognition of that beyond momentary, “well, that was just a one off; I’m fine!” Likewise, I was raised by alcoholic parents and only started realizing how abnormal my childhood was (they were very supportive parents of me, but alcohol was ever present) after getting married 2.5 years ago. I was increasingly aware of the problem, but still was resistant. Until finally I found myself in a situation that forced me to confront the truth, potentially lose my marriage and lose my job in the process.

In one sense this ended up derailing my practice of Druid ritual, setting aside most of the AODA course except for growing my tree and other plants. But I have had such a powerful redemptive awakening. I don’t know how else to liken except as Paul says in Ephesians 5:14 - “Therefore it says, ‘Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you.’” I went from alcoholic to completely broken chains in the span of one series of conversations throughout one day. It’s nothing short of a spiritual resurrection, where I was dead, in a sense, and now I’m alive. And the ensuing process of personal growth, now that alcohol has been removed, has fundamentally changed my life in ways I could not have imagined.

Now I find myself of sane mind in a way I have not known in some 7 or 8 years, my marriage in an incredible shape, healthier than I have ever been, more productive, and freer than I could ever have imagined. And with the summer solstice just passed, I find myself now picking up the practice again, having overcome a seemingly insurmountable obstacle that could have plagued me for years to come.

It’s been a wild year, but it really is like I’ve experience “new creation”—very Christian and Druid at the same time haha

Gratefully, BE

replies.content: I'm delighted to hear this -- but not surprised. Once you start seriously following the Path, these things happen.
